name: Passive Voice
intuition: The semantic patient of the event functions as the grammatical subject. The agent is either expressed in a by-phrase (e.g., 'by John') or omitted entirely. The construction includes overt morphological marking of the passive voice.
set_to_1_if: The gram has the form auxiliary 'have' + 'been' + past participle of a transitive verb (e.g., 'has been eaten', 'had been seen').
The grammatical subject corresponds to the semantic patient (the entity affected by the event), not the agent.
The clause contains a 'by'-phrase expressing the external argument of the target verb (e.g., 'has been written by Paul').

The construction uses 'get/got' as the auxiliary introducing a past participle (e.g., 'got hired', 'has gotten promoted'), indicating a passive interpretation where the subject corresponds to the semantic patient.
set_to_0_if: The grammatical subject corresponds to the semantic agent (doer/initiator) of the target verb, and the grammatical object corresponds to the semantic patient.

The complement of 'been' is a non-verbal predicate (NP/AP/PP), and no past participle verb intervenes between 'been' and that predicate; in these cases 'been' is the lexical copula, not a passive auxiliary.

The subject denotes the undergoer of a change-of-state, but the clause lacks overt passive morphology (i.e., no auxiliary 'be/been' or 'get/got' introducing a past participle); such intransitive alternations (e.g., 'The vase broke') are not passive.
examples_positive: He had been known by all. (Passive + agent in 'by'-phrase -> 1)
examples_negative: The door has been open. (Copular adjective -> 0)
